IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important problem of modeling local text coherence—an area with many downstream applications (essay scoring, story generation, sentence ordering) by neuralizing the widely used entity-grid representation. Its proposed Grid-CNN and Extended Grid-CNN obtain measurable gains over strong baselines (e.g., “absolute improvements of about 4% in discrimination and 1% in insertion” and further gains when adding entity features), and the authors report state-of-the-art results on standard evaluation tasks (discrimination, insertion, summary coherence rating). By showing that distributed representations and convolutional feature learning can improve classic entity-grid models, the work is likely to change how coherence is modeled and to stimulate follow-up neural approaches and wider adoption in downstream systems. | B(4): The paper addresses an important and well-studied problem (local text coherence) by neuralizing the widely-used entity grid representation and shows measurable gains (e.g., ~4% absolute in discrimination and ~2.5% in insertion for Extended Grid-CNN vs. Extended Grid). By demonstrating that convolutional, distributed representations can model long-range entity transitions and incorporate entity-specific features compactly, it is likely to change how coherence models are built and be adopted in related tasks (sentence ordering, summarization, readability). While not a paradigm shift, it opens substantial and practical new directions for neural coherence modeling and downstream applications.

Its proposed Grid-CNN and Extended Grid-CNN obtain measurable gains over strong baselines (e.g., “absolute improvements of about 4% in discrimination and 1% in insertion” and further gains when adding entity features), and the authors report state-of-the-art results on standard evaluation tasks (discrimination, insertion, summary coherence rating). By showing that distributed representations and convolutional feature learning can improve classic entity-grid models, the work is likely to change how coherence is modeled and to stimulate follow-up neural approaches and wider adoption in downstream systems.

By demonstrating that convolutional, distributed representations can model long-range entity transitions and incorporate entity-specific features compactly, it is likely to change how coherence models are built and be adopted in related tasks (sentence ordering, summarization, readability). While not a paradigm shift, it opens substantial and practical new directions for neural coherence modeling and downstream applications.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper neuralizes the established entity-grid framework (Barzilay & Lapata, 2008) by applying convolutional neural networks over grid columns and incorporating entity-specific features (Section 3.1 and claims that it "neuralize[s] the popular entity grid models" and uses "convolutional architecture to model sufficiently long entity transitions"). It explicitly positions itself as different from prior neural work (Li & Hovy, 2014) but mainly adapts known neural techniques (CNNs, distributed representations, pairwise ranking) to an existing representation (Elsner & Charniak, 2011), so the contribution is a solid, non-trivial engineering/representation extension but largely incremental rather than a fundamentally new idea. | B(3): The paper neuralizes the well‑known entity grid by applying distributed representations and a convolutional architecture over the grid to capture long‑range entity transitions (e.g., "we propose to neuralize the popular entity grid models"; "we use convolutional architecture to model sufficiently long entity transitions"). It also embeds entity‑specific features (e.g., encoding Gi,j as PERSON‑S) and trains via pairwise ranking, and explicitly contrasts its approach with Li and Hovy (2014) who operate at the sentence level. This is a clear, non‑trivial adaptation of existing ideas (entity grid + CNNs/embeddings) but is incremental rather than a fundamentally new problem or paradigm.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper neuralizes the established entity-grid framework (Barzilay & Lapata, 2008) by applying convolutional neural networks over grid columns and incorporating entity-specific features (Section 3.1 and claims that it "neuralize[s] the popular entity grid models" and uses "convolutional architecture to model sufficiently long entity transitions"). It explicitly positions itself as different from prior neural work (Li & Hovy, 2014) but mainly adapts known neural techniques (CNNs, distributed representations, pairwise ranking) to an existing representation (Elsner & Charniak, 2011), so the contribution is a solid, non-trivial engineering/representation extension but largely incremental rather than a fundamentally new idea.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper neuralizes the well‑known entity grid by applying distributed representations and a convolutional architecture over the grid to capture long‑range entity transitions (e.g., "we propose to neuralize the popular entity grid models"; "we use convolutional architecture to model sufficiently long entity transitions"). It also embeds entity‑specific features (e.g., encoding Gi,j as PERSON‑S) and trains via pairwise ranking, and explicitly contrasts its approach with Li and Hovy (2014) who operate at the sentence level. This is a clear, non‑trivial adaptation of existing ideas (entity grid + CNNs/embeddings) but is incremental rather than a fundamentally new problem or paradigm.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): Yes — the paper is squarely within top NLP/ML venue scope: it proposes a neural convolutional model over the well-known entity-grid for local coherence. It targets the right audience (discourse/coherence and neural-NLP researchers) and formulates standard tasks (discrimination, insertion, summary coherence rating) using standard datasets (WSJ, DUC'03). Methods and evaluations follow community expectations (Grid-CNN/Extended Grid-CNN, pairwise ranking, comparisons to Elsner & Charniak, Guinaudeau & Strube, Li & Hovy, use of dev/test splits, dropout/optimizers), and the paper is positioned with appropriate related work and baselines. | B(5): This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it neuralizes a core NLP problem (local discourse coherence) by applying a convolutional model over the entity grid (stated in the abstract/introduction and Section 3.1). It targets the right audience and evaluation standards by using canonical coherence tasks (discrimination, insertion, summary coherence rating) and standard datasets (WSJ for sentence ordering and DUC’03 for summary rating; Sections 4 and 5) and compares to established baselines (Barzilay & Lapata, Elsner & Charniak, Guinaudeau & Strube). The methodology (CNNs, pairwise ranking, dropout/optimizers) and positioning within related work (Section 6) follow expectations for top-tier NLP/ML conferences.

reviewer_a.justification: Yes — the paper is squarely within top NLP/ML venue scope: it proposes a neural convolutional model over the well-known entity-grid for local coherence. It targets the right audience (discourse/coherence and neural-NLP researchers) and formulates standard tasks (discrimination, insertion, summary coherence rating) using standard datasets (WSJ, DUC'03). Methods and evaluations follow community expectations (Grid-CNN/Extended Grid-CNN, pairwise ranking, comparisons to Elsner & Charniak, Guinaudeau & Strube, Li & Hovy, use of dev/test splits, dropout/optimizers), and the paper is positioned with appropriate related work and baselines.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it neuralizes a core NLP problem (local discourse coherence) by applying a convolutional model over the entity grid (stated in the abstract/introduction and Section 3.1). It targets the right audience and evaluation standards by using canonical coherence tasks (discrimination, insertion, summary coherence rating) and standard datasets (WSJ for sentence ordering and DUC’03 for summary rating; Sections 4 and 5) and compares to established baselines (Barzilay & Lapata, Elsner & Charniak, Guinaudeau & Strube). The methodology (CNNs, pairwise ranking, dropout/optimizers) and positioning within related work (Section 6) follow expectations for top-tier NLP/ML conferences.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): The paper’s overall structure (background → model → experiments) is present, but presentation is marred by many repetitions, truncations and copy-paste artifacts that break logical flow (e.g., multiple duplicated citation blocks and repeated paragraphs around Centering Theory and entity grid). Main contributions are mentioned (neural convolutional entity-grid model), but not stated cleanly early and are scattered. Notation and detail have inconsistencies and omissions (e.g., ‘4k transitions probabilities’ likely should be 4^k, a parameter list shows ‘(ρ and )’ with a missing symbol). Figures/tables are referenced (Figure 2, Table 3/4) but not provided in the excerpt and several sentences contain grammatical errors or wrong word choices (e.g., “loosing generalization”, “gain in insertion is less verbose”), indicating the manuscript needs careful editing for clarity. | B(2): The paper has a reasonable high-level organization (entity grid → model → experiments → related work), but pervasive editorial and formatting problems significantly hinder clarity. The text contains many duplicated and garbled passages (e.g., multiple repeated paragraphs and fragmented citations in the Related Work and earlier sections), odd phrasing (“gain in insertion is less verbose”), and remnants of LaTeX/parenthetical fragments that interrupt reading. Figures/tables are frequently referenced (Figure 2, Table 3/4) but not shown here, and some terminology/notation (Gi,j) is introduced but later sections include cut-off or inconsistent sentences, making important details hard to follow without major copyediting.

Main contributions are mentioned (neural convolutional entity-grid model), but not stated cleanly early and are scattered. Notation and detail have inconsistencies and omissions (e.g., ‘4k transitions probabilities’ likely should be 4^k, a parameter list shows ‘(ρ and )’ with a missing symbol). Figures/tables are referenced (Figure 2, Table 3/4) but not provided in the excerpt and several sentences contain grammatical errors or wrong word choices (e.g., “loosing generalization”, “gain in insertion is less verbose”), indicating the manuscript needs careful editing for clarity.

The text contains many duplicated and garbled passages (e.g., multiple repeated paragraphs and fragmented citations in the Related Work and earlier sections), odd phrasing (“gain in insertion is less verbose”), and remnants of LaTeX/parenthetical fragments that interrupt reading. Figures/tables are frequently referenced (Figure 2, Table 3/4) but not shown here, and some terminology/notation (Gi,j) is introduced but later sections include cut-off or inconsistent sentences, making important details hard to follow without major copyediting.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (Elsner & Charniak 2011 extended/`all-nouns` grids, Guinaudeau & Strube 2013 graph model, Barzilay & Lapata 2008 for summary task, and attempts Li & Hovy 2014) and uses prior datasets/tasks (WSJ for ordering, DUC'03 for summaries) (Sec. 5.1, 4.2). Comparisons appear fair: baselines were run with standard toolkits/implementations (Brown coherence toolkit, cohere6) on the same data and metrics (discrimination, insertion, summary rating), and the paper reports when prior methods perform best among baselines (Extended Grid) and explains performance differences and limitations (e.g., excluded Li & Hovy reproduction issues; analysis of insertion vs. discrimination gains). The paper also discusses related approaches and explicitly analyzes why their neuralization yields gains (Sect. 5.3, Related Work), so comparisons are comprehensive and reasonably analyzed. | B(5): The paper compares to the appropriate and recent baselines (Barzilay & Lapata 2008 entity grid, Elsner & Charniak 2011 extended grid, Guinaudeau & Strube 2013 graph model and others) and uses the same evaluation tasks and datasets (WSJ/DUC, discrimination/insertion/summary rating) as prior work. Baselines are run with standard toolkits/command-line options (Brown coherence toolkit, cohere6) and metrics, and the authors report when a prior method (Extended Grid) gets the highest non-neural scores and explain relative gains (see §5.1–5.3). They also attempted to include Li & Hovy (2014) and explicitly note/explain exclusion due to poor reproduced results, and they analyze differences between their CNN-over-grid approach and prior models (Related Work, §6). Overall comparisons are comprehensive, fairly executed, and the most relevant competing approaches are cited and discussed.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (Elsner & Charniak 2011 extended/`all-nouns` grids, Guinaudeau & Strube 2013 graph model, Barzilay & Lapata 2008 for summary task, and attempts Li & Hovy 2014) and uses prior datasets/tasks (WSJ for ordering, DUC'03 for summaries) (Sec. 5.1, 4.2). Comparisons appear fair: baselines were run with standard toolkits/implementations (Brown coherence toolkit, cohere6) on the same data and metrics (discrimination, insertion, summary rating), and the paper reports when prior methods perform best among baselines (Extended Grid) and explains performance differences and limitations (e.g., excluded Li & Hovy reproduction issues; analysis of insertion vs. discrimination gains). The paper also discusses related approaches and explicitly analyzes why their neuralization yields gains (Sect. 5.3, Related Work), so comparisons are comprehensive and reasonably analyzed.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares to the appropriate and recent baselines (Barzilay & Lapata 2008 entity grid, Elsner & Charniak 2011 extended grid, Guinaudeau & Strube 2013 graph model and others) and uses the same evaluation tasks and datasets (WSJ/DUC, discrimination/insertion/summary rating) as prior work. Baselines are run with standard toolkits/command-line options (Brown coherence toolkit, cohere6) and metrics, and the authors report when a prior method (Extended Grid) gets the highest non-neural scores and explain relative gains (see §5.1–5.3). They also attempted to include Li & Hovy (2014) and explicitly note/explain exclusion due to poor reproduced results, and they analyze differences between their CNN-over-grid approach and prior models (Related Work, §6). Overall comparisons are comprehensive, fairly executed, and the most relevant competing approaches are cited and discussed.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The experiments cover the standard coherence benchmarks (discrimination, insertion on WSJ; summary coherence on DUC’03) and compare against several strong baselines including the Extended Grid, reporting concrete relative gains (e.g. ~4% discrimination, 2.5% insertion). However, there are no thorough ablation or sensitivity analyses (beyond comparing Grid-CNN vs Extended Grid-CNN) for model components, hyperparameters, or feature choices, and the paper admits it did not train specifically for insertion. Related work is well surveyed, but discussion of limitations/failure cases is minimal (only brief notes about insertion difficulty and a failed Li & Hovy replication). Reproducibility is only partially supported (toolkit commands, optimizer and training regime mentioned) but key architecture and hyperparameter details and exact result tables are not present in the provided text. | B(3): The experiments cover standard coherence evaluation tasks (discrimination, insertion, summary rating) on accepted datasets (WSJ for ordering, DUC’03 for summaries) and compare to reasonable baselines including the Extended Grid and graph-based methods (Sections 4–5). However, there is no systematic ablation or sensitivity analysis (e.g., contribution of each entity-specific feature, filter sizes, embedding/hyperparameter choices) beyond comparing Grid-CNN vs Extended Grid-CNN, and the paper admits not training specifically for insertion and excludes the Li & Hovy model results (Section 5). Related work is discussed broadly and fairly (Section 6), but failure modes and limitations are only briefly noted (difficulty on insertion, excluded negative result) rather than analyzed in depth. Reproducibility is partially supported by training procedure details and toolkit references, but key architectural/hyperparameter and preprocessing/coreference specifics are missing, limiting exact replication.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments cover the standard coherence benchmarks (discrimination, insertion on WSJ; summary coherence on DUC’03) and compare against several strong baselines including the Extended Grid, reporting concrete relative gains (e.g. ~4% discrimination, 2.5% insertion). However, there are no thorough ablation or sensitivity analyses (beyond comparing Grid-CNN vs Extended Grid-CNN) for model components, hyperparameters, or feature choices, and the paper admits it did not train specifically for insertion. Related work is well surveyed, but discussion of limitations/failure cases is minimal (only brief notes about insertion difficulty and a failed Li & Hovy replication). Reproducibility is only partially supported (toolkit commands, optimizer and training regime mentioned) but key architecture and hyperparameter details and exact result tables are not present in the provided text.

However, there is no systematic ablation or sensitivity analysis (e.g., contribution of each entity-specific feature, filter sizes, embedding/hyperparameter choices) beyond comparing Grid-CNN vs Extended Grid-CNN, and the paper admits not training specifically for insertion and excludes the Li & Hovy model results (Section 5). Related work is discussed broadly and fairly (Section 6), but failure modes and limitations are only briefly noted (difficulty on insertion, excluded negative result) rather than analyzed in depth. Reproducibility is partially supported by training procedure details and toolkit references, but key architectural/hyperparameter and preprocessing/coreference specifics are missing, limiting exact replication.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The central claim—that a CNN-based model over the entity-grid yields improved coherence scores—is clearly stated and supported by experiments on standard tasks (discrimination, insertion, DUC’03 summary rating) with reported absolute gains (e.g. ≈4% discrimination, ≈1–2.5% insertion). The authors compare against appropriate baselines implemented with established toolkits (Brown coherence, cohere6) and use standard datasets (WSJ, DUC’03) and training procedures (dropout, early stopping). Minor technical concerns remain: no statistical significance testing is reported, the summary test set is small (80 pairs), hyperparameter/tuning parity between neural and non-neural baselines is not fully detailed, and the extended-grid comparisons use different feature sets (their CNN uses only 3 of 9 features), which complicates a strict apples-to-apples comparison. These issues are not fatal to the main conclusion but justify a minor deduction. | B(4): The paper states clear claims (a CNN-based neuralization of the entity-grid with pairwise ranking) and supports them with experiments on standard tasks/datasets and reasonable baselines (Grid-all nouns, Extended Grid (E&C), Guinaudeau & Strube graph model; WSJ for ordering and DUC’03 for summary rating) as described in Sections 4–5. Reported gains (e.g. ≈4% discrimination and ≈1–2.5% insertion improvements; Extended Grid-CNN outperforming Extended Grid) indicate the model is effective. However, the excerpt omits numerical tables, significance testing, and some implementation/ablation details (exact architecture/filter sizes, full hyperparameters and complete results), and the authors do not report statistical significance or deeper error analysis, which are minor reporting gaps that do not overturn the experimental claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The central claim—that a CNN-based model over the entity-grid yields improved coherence scores—is clearly stated and supported by experiments on standard tasks (discrimination, insertion, DUC’03 summary rating) with reported absolute gains (e.g. ≈4% discrimination, ≈1–2.5% insertion). The authors compare against appropriate baselines implemented with established toolkits (Brown coherence, cohere6) and use standard datasets (WSJ, DUC’03) and training procedures (dropout, early stopping). Minor technical concerns remain: no statistical significance testing is reported, the summary test set is small (80 pairs), hyperparameter/tuning parity between neural and non-neural baselines is not fully detailed, and the extended-grid comparisons use different feature sets (their CNN uses only 3 of 9 features), which complicates a strict apples-to-apples comparison. These issues are not fatal to the main conclusion but justify a minor deduction.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear claims (a CNN-based neuralization of the entity-grid with pairwise ranking) and supports them with experiments on standard tasks/datasets and reasonable baselines (Grid-all nouns, Extended Grid (E&C), Guinaudeau & Strube graph model; WSJ for ordering and DUC’03 for summary rating) as described in Sections 4–5. Reported gains (e.g. ≈4% discrimination and ≈1–2.5% insertion improvements; Extended Grid-CNN outperforming Extended Grid) indicate the model is effective. However, the excerpt omits numerical tables, significance testing, and some implementation/ablation details (exact architecture/filter sizes, full hyperparameters and complete results), and the authors do not report statistical significance or deeper error analysis, which are minor reporting gaps that do not overturn the experimental claims.
